questions.stem: Two sets $A$ and $B$ are equal precisely when:
options: $A$ and $B$ have the same number of elements.
questions.explanation: By the axiom of extensionality, $A = B$ means they have exactly the same elements, which is equivalent to mutual inclusion: $A \subseteq B$ and $B \subseteq A$. Having the same cardinality is far weaker (it does not require the elements to match).
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: One of De Morgan's laws states that the complement of a union is:
questions.explanation: De Morgan's law gives $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$: a point is outside $A \cup B$ iff it is outside both $A$ and $B$. The dual law is $(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: For arbitrary indexed families, the general De Morgan law reads:
questions.explanation: Complementation turns arbitrary unions into intersections: $\left(\bigcup_\alpha A_\alpha\right)^c = \bigcap_\alpha A_\alpha^c$. A point misses the whole union iff it misses every member, i.e. it lies in every complement.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $B = \{2, 3, 4\}$. What is $A \setminus B$?
questions.explanation: The difference $A \setminus B$ keeps the elements of $A$ that are not in $B$. Only $1$ lies in $A$ but not in $B$, so $A \setminus B = \{1\}$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The distributive law for sets states that $A \cap (B \cup C)$ equals:
questions.explanation: Intersection distributes over union: $A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)$. Dually, union distributes over intersection, giving $A \cup (B \cap C) = (A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C)$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A relation $R$ on a set $X$ is an equivalence relation if it is:
questions.explanation: An equivalence relation must be reflexive ($x \sim x$), symmetric ($x \sim y \Rightarrow y \sim x$), and transitive ($x \sim y, y \sim z \Rightarrow x \sim z$). Antisymmetry instead characterizes partial orders.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The equivalence classes of an equivalence relation on $X$:
options: are all equal to each other.
questions.explanation: Distinct equivalence classes are disjoint and their union is all of $X$, so the classes partition $X$. Conversely, every partition of $X$ arises from a unique equivalence relation.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Consider $\sim$ on $\mathbb{Z}$ defined by $a \sim b$ iff $a - b$ is even. This relation is:
options: an equivalence relation with two classes.
an equivalence relation with infinitely many classes.
questions.explanation: Congruence modulo $2$ is an equivalence relation, and it splits $\mathbb{Z}$ into exactly two classes: the even integers and the odd integers. So there are $2$ equivalence classes.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A function $f : X \to Y$ is injective (one-to-one) when:
options: $f$ has an inverse on all of $Y$.
every $y \in Y$ is hit by some $x$.
questions.explanation: Injectivity means distinct inputs give distinct outputs, equivalently $f(x_1) = f(x_2) \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2$. Option C is just the definition of a well-defined function and holds automatically.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A function $f : X \to Y$ is surjective (onto) when:
questions.explanation: Surjectivity means every element of the codomain is attained: $f(X) = Y$. Equivalently, for each $y \in Y$ there exists $x \in X$ with $f(x) = y$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A bijection $f : X \to Y$ is a function that is:
questions.explanation: A bijection is both one-to-one and onto, so it pairs the elements of $X$ with those of $Y$ perfectly. This is exactly the condition for $f$ to have a two-sided inverse $f^{-1} : Y \to X$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: For $f : X \to Y$ and subsets $A, B \subseteq X$, which identity always holds for the image?
questions.explanation: Images always respect unions: $f(A \cup B) = f(A) \cup f(B)$. The other three can fail for the image (only $\subseteq$ holds for intersection in general); preimages, by contrast, respect all set operations.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: For $f : X \to Y$ and $C, D \subseteq Y$, the preimage satisfies which exact identity?
questions.explanation: Preimages commute with all Boolean operations: $f^{-1}(C \cap D) = f^{-1}(C) \cap f^{-1}(D)$, $f^{-1}(C \cup D) = f^{-1}(C) \cup f^{-1}(D)$, and $f^{-1}(C^c) = f^{-1}(C)^c$. This good behaviour is why continuity is phrased via preimages.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Let $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, $f(x) = x^2$. The preimage $f^{-1}(\{4\})$ is:
questions.explanation: The preimage collects all $x$ with $x^2 = 4$, namely $x = 2$ and $x = -2$. So $f^{-1}(\{4\}) = \{-2, 2\}$, which incidentally shows $f$ is not injective.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A set $A$ is countable (in Rudin's sense of countably infinite) when:
options: there is a bijection between $A$ and $\mathbb{R}$.
there is a bijection between $A$ and $\mathbb{N}$.
questions.explanation: A set is countably infinite when it can be put in one-to-one correspondence with $\mathbb{N}$, i.e. its elements can be listed as a sequence $a_1, a_2, a_3, \dots$. A bijection with $\mathbb{R}$ would make it uncountable.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers is:
questions.explanation: The rationals are countable: arranging fractions $p/q$ in a grid and traversing diagonally lists them as a sequence. So $\mathbb{Q}$ has the same cardinality as $\mathbb{N}$, despite being dense in $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Cantor's diagonal argument proves that:
questions.explanation: Cantor's diagonal argument shows $\mathbb{R}$ (already $[0,1]$) is uncountable: any proposed list of reals can be defeated by constructing a number differing from the $n$-th listed number in the $n$-th decimal place. Hence no bijection with $\mathbb{N}$ exists.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A countable union of countable sets is:
questions.explanation: A countable union of countable sets is countable (a standard consequence of arranging the elements in an array and listing diagonally). This is how one shows $\mathbb{Q} = \bigcup_n \{ p/n : p \in \mathbb{Z} \}$ is countable.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Which of these sets is uncountable?
options: the set of all subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ (the power set).
the set of finite subsets of $\mathbb{N}$.
questions.explanation: By Cantor's theorem, $\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$ has strictly larger cardinality than $\mathbb{N}$, so it is uncountable. The integers, $\mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N}$, and the finite subsets of $\mathbb{N}$ are all countable.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: If $f : X \to Y$ is injective and $X$ is infinite, then $f(X)$ is:
questions.explanation: An injection restricts to a bijection from $X$ onto its image $f(X)$, so $f(X)$ has the same cardinality as $X$ and is therefore infinite. It need not equal $Y$ (that would require surjectivity).
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: For a function $f : X \to Y$, which inclusion always holds for any $A \subseteq X$?
questions.explanation: Every point of $A$ maps into $f(A)$, so it lies in $f^{-1}(f(A))$; thus $A \subseteq f^{-1}(f(A))$. Equality holds for all $A$ exactly when $f$ is injective.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The power set $\mathcal{P}(X)$ of a finite set $X$ with $n$ elements has how many elements?
questions.explanation: Each element of $X$ is either in or out of a given subset, giving $2 \cdot 2 \cdots 2 = 2^n$ subsets. For example a $3$-element set has $2^3 = 8$ subsets.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Two sets have the same cardinality if and only if:
options: one is a subset of the other.
there exists a bijection between them.
questions.explanation: Equinumerosity is defined by the existence of a bijection between the sets. This notion extends counting to infinite sets, where, for instance, $\mathbb{N}$ and $\mathbb{Z}$ have the same cardinality.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Which statement is TRUE?
options: Every subset of a countable set is countable (finite or countably infinite).
questions.explanation: Every subset of a countable set is countable (finite or countably infinite). The others are false: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable while $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable, $(0,1)$ is uncountable, and no bijection $\mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}$ exists.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Let $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ and $B = \{3, 4, 5\}$. Compute the basic set operations.
questions.solution: The intersection $A \cap B = \{3, 4\}$ has cardinality {{1}}. The union $A \cup B = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ has cardinality {{2}}. The difference $A \setminus B$ keeps the elements of $A$ not in $B$, so the number of elements in $A \setminus B$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $A \cap B = \{3,4\}$ has $2$ elements; $A \cup B = \{1,2,3,4,5\}$ has $5$; and $A \setminus B = \{1,2\}$ has $2$ elements. Note $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B| = 4 + 3 - 2 = 5$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Define $\sim$ on $\mathbb{Z}$ by $a \sim b$ iff $a \equiv b \pmod 3$ (that is, $3$ divides $a - b$).
questions.solution: This relation is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, so it {{1}} an equivalence relation. The equivalence classes are the residue sets modulo $3$, so the number of distinct classes is {{2}}. The class of $7$ is the set of integers congruent to $7$, and since $7 = 3 \cdot 2 + 1$, that class is the same as the class of the remainder {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Congruence modulo $3$ is an equivalence relation with three classes (remainders $0, 1, 2$). Since $7 \equiv 1 \pmod 3$, the class of $7$ equals the class of $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Consider $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ given by $f(x) = x^2$, together with its image and preimage behaviour.
questions.solution: The preimage $f^{-1}(\{9\})$ consists of all $x$ with $x^2 = 9$, so the number of points in it is {{1}}. Because two different inputs can share an output, the function $f$ {{2}} injective on $\mathbb{R}$. Since negative numbers are never values of $x^2$, the function $f$ {{3}} surjective onto $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explanation: $x^2 = 9$ gives $x = \pm 3$, so the preimage has $2$ points. Sharing outputs ($f(3) = f(-3)$) shows $f$ is not injective, and the range $[0, \infty)$ misses negatives, so $f$ is not surjective onto $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Classify each set as countable or uncountable, then count how many of them are countable.
questions.solution: The set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rationals {{1}}, the set $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$ {{2}}, while the real interval $(0,1)$ is uncountable by Cantor's diagonal argument. Among the three sets $\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, and $(0,1)$, the number that are countable is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable and $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$ is countable (a product of two countable sets). The interval $(0,1)$ is uncountable. So $2$ of the three sets are countable.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis
